title: Kohn-Sham Hamiltonian from Effective Field Theory: Quasiparticle Band Narrowing from Frozen Core Dynamics
authors: Xiansheng Cai, Han Wang, Kun Chen
abstract: Kohn-Sham (KS) eigenvalues are routinely compared with angle-resolved photoemission (ARPES) and used as input for many-body methods, yet density functional theory (DFT) assigns them no physical meaning. For alkali and alkaline-earth metals, KS bandwidths overestimate ARPES measurements by 20-35%, a discrepancy that persists across all exchange-correlation functionals. We construct an effective field theory (EFT) of the inhomogeneous electron gas and show that two conditions imply KS bands are the quasiparticle bands, up to a frozen-core renormalization factor zcore: a scale separation between core excitation energies and the valence Fermi energy, and an approximate Galilean invariance of the uniform electron gas confirmed by diagrammatic Monte Carlo. This factor reflects dynamical core excitations that conventional pseudopotentials freeze out and no static potential can capture. The correction 1-zcore reaches 20-35% for alkali metals but falls below 5% for Al and Si, explaining both the failure and success of KS band theory. We derive a closed-form post-SCF formula and validate it for Li, Na, K, Ca, Mg, Al, and Si; the predicted quasiparticle bands resolve the long-standing ARPES bandwidth discrepancy, matching embedded dynamical mean-field theory at negligible cost. This work also exemplifies first-principles agentic science, a direction particularly suited to the AGI-for-Science paradigm: an LLM-co-developed derivation with controlled approximations, verified symbolically and against a few experiments, becomes a deterministic harness for agentic scale-out, resolving simultaneously the LLM audit bottleneck and the non-falsifiability of fit-based AI-for-science.
summary: This paper addresses the long-standing discrepancy between DFT-calculated Kohn-Sham bands and experimental ARPES data in simple metals. By using an Effective Field Theory to account for the dynamical effects of core electrons that are usually 'frozen' in standard pseudopotentials, the authors derive a correction factor that narrows the predicted bandwidth. This approach successfully matches experimental results for several elements and demonstrates how LLM-based agents can be used to reconstruct and verify fundamental many-body theories.
main_problem: The systematic overestimation of bandwidth in alkali and alkaline-earth metals by Kohn-Sham eigenvalues compared to ARPES measurements, caused by the neglect of dynamical core-electron excitations in standard DFT.
main_result: The derivation of an Effective Field Theory (EFT) that introduces a renormalization factor (z_core) to the Kohn-Sham bands, successfully resolving the bandwidth discrepancy by accounting for frozen-core dynamics.
method: Construction of an EFT for the inhomogeneous electron gas using a dual-fermion approach and Renormalized Perturbation Theory (RPT) to integrate out core electrons.
model_or_system: The system consists of sp-bonded metals (Li, Na, K, Mg, Ca, Al, Si) modeled via a Born-Oppenheimer action and a Kohn-Sham Hamiltonian augmented by an energy-dependent pseudopotential.
key_observables: Quasipability bandwidth, quasiparticle residue (z), effective mass (m*), and the frozen-core renormalization factor (z_core).
important_parameters: Core excitation energy (Delta E_c), core form factor (f_c^K), and the scale separation ratio (epsilon_F / Delta E_c).
main_assumptions: Assumes scale separation between core and valence energies, approximate Galilean invariance of the uniform electron gas, and the validity of the Born-Oppenheimer approximation.
figures_summary: Comparison of LDA and PBE band structures with and without EFT corrections for various elements; validation of the Li BCC valence band structure using different pseudopotentials.
paper_structure: Introduction of the bandwidth discrepancy; derivation of the EFT and the energy-dependent pseudopotential; application of Renormalized Perturbation Theory; numerical validation against ARPES and DMFT; discussion of the 'Agentic Science' paradigm for theory construction.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Valence bands compared with ARPES data. Solid: LDA KS (blue) and QP (red). Dotted: PBE KS and QP. Na and K along N–Γ–N: Expt. 1 = Ref. [2], Expt. 2 = Ref. [3], K expt. = Ref. [4]. Mg along Γ–A–Γ: expt. = Ref. [5]. Al along Γ–X: expt. = Ref. [6]. PBE closely tracks LDA, confirming robustness to the functional.

FIG. 2. Dimensionless ratio f c K/∆Ec for the dominant core channel of each element. This ratio controls zcore [Eq. (3)]: elements with large |f c/∆Ec| have large corrections. K and Li have the largest ratios because ∆Ec is small (1.7 to 2.6 Ha) while |f c K| ∼Jc ∼1 to 2 Ha is comparable. The [Ne]-core se- ries Na–Mg–Al–Si shows monotonically decreasing amplitude: |f c K| is nearly the same for all four because Jc and the orbital size ⟨r⟩c scale inversely with Z, keeping |fc| ∼Jc⟨r⟩c ∼1 Ha; meanwhile ∆E2s roughly triples from Na to Si. The sign change near K ≈2 to 3 Bohr−1 leads to partial cancellation in the coherent sum F c νk.

FIG. 3. Quasiparticle properties of the UEG at rs = 4 from DiagMC. Left: z(k) = 1/(1−∂ωRe Σ) is nearly constant within the Fermi sea. Right: [ϵk −∆Re Σ(k)]/ϵk (where ϵk = k2/2m and ∆Σ(k) = Σ(k) −Σ(0)) is likewise flat, confirming ∂ωΣ + ∂ϵΣ ≈0 and hence m∗≈m.

FIG. 4. RPT self-energy diagrams to second order. (a) KS propagator. (b,c) Two- and three-point counterterms. (d) First- order Fock residual. (e) RPA/bubble. (f) SOX/charge-vertex. (g) Nested Fock. Solid boxes (red): P Γ 2 subtraction; dashed boxes (blue): P 0 3 subtraction.
FIG. 5. LDA vs PBE band structures with EFT quasiparticle correction for seven elements. Solid: LDA; dashed: PBE. Blue: KS; red: QP.
FIG. 6. Li BCC band structure along N–Γ–H. Blue: KS; red: EFT QP. Solid: GTH-LDA (Zv = 1); dashed: EFT nonlocal KB PSP; dotted: PBE (Zv = 3).
